title: Infrastructure Core and Philanthropy
subtitle: A University of Iowa support structure for global SignWriting infrastructure
summary: A University of Iowa-facing presentation framing the Sutton SignWriting Infrastructure Core as a philanthropic opportunity that complements the lab-home, operational-value, and grant-development structure.
synopsis: The long-term infrastructure conversation becomes clearer for development, institutional strategy, and philanthropic audiences when it starts with what already exists. Sutton SignWriting already functions as globally used language infrastructure, the current support model is too fragile, and a focused philanthropic investment at the University of Iowa could establish a stronger core for staffing, global coordination, translation and access work, and future research integration.

links.label: Sutton SignWriting
links.description: Overview of Sutton SignWriting as a writing system and global ecosystem.

links.label: University of Iowa
links.description: Institutional home proposed for the infrastructure core.

slides.title: Sutton SignWriting Infrastructure Core and Philanthropy
slides.summary: An already-real global language infrastructure can become more stable through stronger institutional support.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for Infrastructure Core and Philanthropy title slide.
slides.speech: The practical opportunity is simple: take something that already exists, already matters globally, and give it stronger long-term support.
slides.notes: Open calmly and frame the opportunity before moving into scale or need.

slides.title: What This Is
slides.summary: The proposal is not about inventing a new system, but about stabilizing an existing, globally used infrastructure through an institutional core.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what this proposal is.
slides.speech: This is not about creating something from scratch. It is about stabilizing something that already exists.
slides.notes: Keep the distinction between invention and stabilization very clear.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Existing Global Use] --> B[Fragile Maintenance]
  B --> C[Institutional Core]
  C --> D[Stronger Support]

slides.title: What SignWriting Is
slides.summary: SignWriting is a writing system for signed languages that enables literacy, structure, research use, and machine-readable data.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what SignWriting is.
slides.speech: SignWriting makes signed language writable. That changes what becomes possible in education, research, and data.
slides.notes: Orient quickly for listeners who are new to SignWriting.

slides.title: Why It Matters
slides.summary: SignWriting matters across literacy, accessibility, language science, and the data infrastructure needed to support signed language text.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for why SignWriting matters.
slides.speech: It touches multiple domains at once, which is why it belongs in an infrastructure conversation.
slides.notes: Keep the framing broad, credible, and concise.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[SignWriting] --> B[Literacy]
  A --> C[Accessibility]
  A --> D[Linguistics]
  A --> E[Data Infrastructure]

slides.title: The Fragility Problem
slides.summary: The system is already real and useful, which is exactly why dependence on too few maintainers and no strong home has become a maintenance risk.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the fragility problem.
slides.speech: The system already matters. That is exactly why fragility is now a problem.
slides.notes: Create urgency without dramatizing beyond the facts.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Real Use] --> B[Few Maintainers]
  B --> C[Fragility]
  C --> D[Risk to Support]

slides.title: The Opportunity
slides.summary: A focused infrastructure core offers a small, high-leverage intervention that can provide support and unlock broader research growth.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the infrastructure opportunity.
slides.speech: This is a small, high-leverage intervention.
slides.notes: Let the scale feel disciplined rather than grandiose.

slides.title: What the Core Includes
slides.summary: The proposed core would support standards, software, datasets, translation and access, and global coordination as one integrated support unit.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what the infrastructure core includes.
slides.speech: The Infrastructure Core would provide coordinated support across standards, software, data, translation and access, and coordination rather than treating each as a separate problem. This matters because some of the most important SignWriting work already exists but remains hard to reach across language barriers and uneven digital access.
slides.notes: Make the core feel concrete and operational.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Infrastructure Core] --> B[Standards]
  A --> C[Software]
  A --> D[Datasets]
  A --> E[Translation and Access]
  A --> F[Global Coordination]

slides.title: Translation and Access Program
slides.summary: A rights-aware translation program could make especially important SignWriting works accessible in English while returning useful editions to original authors and communities.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for translation and access program.
slides.speech: One especially practical function of the Infrastructure Core would be a translation and access program. The goal would not be to translate everything. It would be to identify high-value works, secure permission, produce AI-assisted translations with human editorial review, and then make those translations available in ways the original authors and communities support. That could include online hosting, print editions, or co-publication.
slides.notes: Keep this program selective, rights-aware, and collaborative rather than extractive.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Priority Work] --> B[Permission and Rights]
  B --> C[AI-Assisted Translation]
  C --> D[Human Review]
  D --> E[Online or Print Access]

slides.title: Staffing Model
slides.summary: The staffing plan is intentionally focused: technical leadership, PI partnership, operational support, and research assistance.
slides.speech: This is not a large center. It is a focused team with a realistic scope.

slides.title: Global Layer
slides.summary: Country ambassadors extend the core by building local relationships, cultural adaptation, and international access around the institutional home.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the global ambassador layer.
slides.speech: The ambassador layer gives the core real international reach through local relationships, cultural adaptation, and on-the-ground institutional awareness.
slides.notes: Keep the model practical and relationship-based.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Core Team] --> B[Country Ambassadors]
  B --> C[Universities]
  B --> D[Communities]
  B --> E[Researchers]

slides.title: Philanthropy Role
slides.summary: Philanthropy is especially well suited to provide stability and long-horizon support where grants alone do not cover the full maintenance burden.
slides.speech: Philanthropy is uniquely powerful at this point in the structure.
slides.notes: Position philanthropy as complementary and strategic.

slides.title: What a Gift Enables
slides.summary: A significant gift can establish core staffing, support, global coordination, translation and access capacity, and the shared infrastructure needed for lasting research readiness.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what a gift enables.
slides.speech: A gift at the right scale can create staffing, support, coordination, translation and access capacity, and event support that can hold over time rather than operating as a short-term patch. This is part of what makes philanthropy especially valuable here: it can support connective infrastructure that grants often leave unfunded.
slides.notes: Keep the tone concrete and future-facing.

slides.title: Symposium
slides.summary: An annual SignWriting symposium would create visibility, legitimacy, and a recurring international gathering point for research, education, and community use.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for annual symposium concept.
slides.speech: A symposium creates visibility, collaboration, and legitimacy while making the infrastructure feel publicly real and globally connected.
slides.notes: Treat the symposium as a multiplier, not a side event.

slides.title: Layered Model
slides.summary: The strongest structure is layered: lab home, operational value, grant development, and philanthropy working together rather than competing.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the layered support model.
slides.speech: No single lane has to do everything. This is layered and realistic.
slides.notes: Connect this deck back to the rest of the University of Iowa series.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Lab Home] --> B[Operational Value]
  B --> C[Grants]
  C --> D[Philanthropy]
  D --> E[Long-Term Support]

slides.title: Why Iowa
slides.summary: The University of Iowa is well positioned because the proposal aligns accessibility, linguistics, research data infrastructure, and collaboration.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for why Iowa fits.
slides.speech: The University of Iowa is uniquely positioned because this work lives at the intersection of accessibility, language research, education, and research infrastructure.
slides.notes: Make the institutional fit feel natural and specific.

slides.title: The Outcome
slides.summary: With support, the result is stronger infrastructure, global impact, and long-term support that can outlast any single individual or moment.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the long-term outcome.
slides.notes: Keep the close focused on durability and value.

slides.title: Closing
slides.summary: The closing argument is that this is a rare chance to stabilize and extend a working global system before fragility causes avoidable loss.
slides.speech: This is a rare opportunity to take something that already works and make sure it stays supported for decades.
slides.notes: End with calm confidence rather than urgency.
